title: Prompt Templates
concept: Parameterized prompt strings with variable slots for reusable, maintainable prompt design.

exercises.prompt: As we scale up to building production LLM applications, how should we manage our prompts?

options.text: By manually copying and pasting text into web chat windows forever.
By treating prompts as reusable code templates with variable inputs.
By letting the LLM write its own code without developer supervision.

exercises.explain: In LLM engineering, we want a structured way to manage prompts as code assets. We are moving from simple conversational testing to building scalable, reusable LLM systems.

cards.title: Stop Copy-Pasting Prompts
cards.body: Have you ever found yourself copy-pasting the exact same prompt instructions over and over, only changing one or two words? Prompt templates solve this by turning your prompts into reusable code functions.

cards.title: What is a Prompt Template?
cards.body: A prompt template is a reusable text string that contains placeholder variables (like {user_name} or {topic}). Before sending the prompt to an LLM, you inject real data into these placeholders.

cards.title: The Party Invitation Template
cards.body: Imagine a form letter for a birthday party invitation. Instead of writing a new invitation from scratch, you write a template with blank spaces for the guest name, date, and location. You just fill in the blanks!

cards.title: Templates in Action
cards.body: Instead of hardcoding 'Write a poem about dogs', you create a template: 'Write a poem about {topic}'. When a user inputs 'cats', your code fills the slot to output 'Write a poem about cats'.

cards.title: Do Templates Block Creativity?
cards.body: You might think templates make prompts rigid or less creative. In reality, templates give you more control over the format and structure, while still letting the LLM be creative with the variables you insert.

cards.title: Hardcoded vs. Templated
cards.body: Compare a messy, hardcoded prompt structure with a clean, templated prompt structure.

left.text: 'Write a recipe for apple pie' (Must rewrite the whole string for cherry pie)

right.text: 'Write a recipe for {dessert}' (Just swap out the dessert variable)

exercises.prompt: In standard Python or LangChain prompting, how is a template placeholder variable typically formatted?
exercises.explain: Dynamic placeholders in templating systems are commonly wrapped in curly braces (like {topic}) to distinguish them from standard static text.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using a prompt template allows you to reuse the same instruction structure for hundreds of different user inputs.
exercises.explain: Prompt templates are highly reusable because you write the core instruction once and can insert any input variable values at runtime.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the definition of how templates handle variables.
exercises.sentence: Prompt templates use placeholders as variable slots that are filled with actual text later.
exercises.explain: Variables or placeholders act as dynamic slots in a prompt template that get filled with real data at runtime.

exercises.prompt: Identify the code snippet that correctly demonstrates a reusable prompt template with variable slots.
exercises.explain: The AI-generated template defines placeholders ({language} and {text}) and formats them dynamically with user input, separating structure from content.

exercises.prompt: Why is using a parameterized prompt template considered a software engineering best practice compared to manually gluing strings together?

options.text: It separates reusable instruction logic from dynamic user input data.
It forces the model to run faster by using less memory.
It automatically translates the prompt into multiple languages.
It encrypts the user's data before sending it to the model.

exercises.explain: Prompt templates separate the fixed instructions (the template structure) from the dynamic input variables. This keeps the prompt reusable and clean, avoiding manual string concatenation clutter in your main codebase.
